title: Separate raw-SQLite ingestion state with retention compaction
description: Persist review and recovery state without opening LEDGER storage or retaining source payloads longer than required.
decision: Use a separate owner-only ingest.db under the Tally data root with Microsoft.Data.Sqlite, snake_case tables, foreign keys, WAL, synchronous FULL, bounded busy handling, PRAGMA user_version migrations, and explicit transactions. Persist immutable manifest revisions, approvals, frozen candidate requests, reconciliation controls, and candidate receipts while incomplete. After verified completion, atomically compact sensitive manifest data to a metadata-only receipt; retain that receipt until explicit cleanup. After an interrupted process releases its batch lock, explicit abandon preserves prior LEDGER effects and retains a terminal metadata tombstone. Safe startup cleanup removes only unlocked stale INGEST lock or atomic-publication artifacts that durable state proves disposable. Normal extraction is memory-only and creates no temporary.
rationale: A separate store makes C2 enforceable and lets A4 retention differ from Durable Ledger State. Sharing LedgerDb or tables creates private coupling. Flat JSON files make candidate-granular transactional recovery and concurrent state checks fragile. A distributed transaction across INGEST and LEDGER does not exist; public idempotency closes the honest cross-store gap.
alternatives: A: share LedgerDb or LEDGER tables, rejected by C2 and retention coupling. B: JSON files per batch, rejected because atomic candidate receipts, migrations, queries, and concurrency become fragile. C: separate raw-SQLite INGEST state, chosen. D: PostgreSQL or hosted store, rejected by C1.
consequences: INGEST and LEDGER cannot share an atomic database transaction. The design relies visibly on stable LEDGER idempotency and INGEST receipt durability. Sensitive incomplete state is queryable and recoverable; completed state is compacted and may be explicitly removed.
status_notes: Accepted design. Separate state, migrations, retention, and cleanup paths are explicit greenfield plan obligations.
